subject: Physics
q: A wire of resistance $R$ and radius $r$ is stretched till its radius became $r / 2$. If new resistance of the stretched wire is $x R$, then value of $x$ is _______
question: A wire of resistance $R$ and radius $r$ is stretched till its radius became $r / 2$. If new resistance of the stretched wire is $x R$, then value of $x$ is _______
solution:
We know $\mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho l}{\mathrm{~A}}, \mathrm{R} \propto \frac{l}{\mathrm{r}^2}$
As we starch the wire, its length will increase but its radius will decrease keeping the volume constant
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{i}}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{f}} \\
& \pi \mathrm{r}^2 l=\pi \frac{\mathrm{r}^2}{4} l_{\mathrm{f}} \\
& l_{\mathrm{f}}=4 l \\
& \frac{\mathrm{R}_{\text {new }}}{\mathrm{R}_{\text {old }}}=\left(\frac{4 l}{\frac{\mathrm{r}^2}{4}}\right) \frac{\mathrm{r}^2}{l}=16 \\
& \mathrm{R}_{\text {new }}=16 \mathrm{R} \\
\therefore & \mathrm{x}=16
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following statements is false?
question: Which of the following statements is false?
options: Kirchhoff's second law represents energy conservation
Wheatstone bridge is the most sensitive when all the four resistances are of the same order of magnitude
In a balanced Wheatstone bridge if the cell and the galvanometer are exchanged, the null point is disturbed
A rheostat can be used as a potential divider
solution:
Let us assume R1, R2, R3 & R4 is connected balanced wheat stone bridge, such that R1R3=R2R4  ...1 as shown below,

 
                                                                   
If galvanometer and cell is exchanged, then circuit becomes as shown in below figure, 


                                                                        

The circuit is redrawn and can be visualised as below figure, 



                                                                     
In this case, if it is balanced, then there must be, 
R1R2=R3R4  ...2

Equation 1 & 2 realises that, both equations are same, which means null point is not affected.

q: A conducting wire of length l, area of crosssection A and electric resistivity ρ is connected between the terminals of a battery. A potential difference V is developed between its ends, causing an electric current. If the length of the wire of the same material is doubled and the area of cross-section is halved, the resultant current would be___
question: A conducting wire of length l, area of crosssection A and electric resistivity ρ is connected between the terminals of a battery. A potential difference V is developed between its ends, causing an electric current. If the length of the wire of the same material is doubled and the area of cross-section is halved, the resultant current would be___
solution:
As per the question 

Resistance =ρ2lA/2=4ρl A

⇒ Current =VR=VA4ρl

q: The difference of temperature in a material can convert heat energy into electrical energy. To harvest the heat energy, the material should have
question: The difference of temperature in a material can convert heat energy into electrical energy. To harvest the heat energy, the material should have
options: high thermal conductivity and high electrical conductivity
low thermal conductivity and low electrical conductivity
high thermal conductivity and low electrical conductivity
low thermal conductivity and high electrical conductivity

subject: Physics
q: If a copper wire is stretched to increase its length by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The percentage increase in resistance of the wire is _____%.
question: If a copper wire is stretched to increase its length by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The percentage increase in resistance of the wire is _____%.
solution:
Before heating, resistance of wire is R0=ρl0A0

After heating, resistance is R=ρlA

As volume = constant

Therefore,

A0l0=1.2l0A

⇒A=A01.2

So,

R=ρlA=ρ×1.2l0A01.2=ρl0A0×1.44=1.44 R0

Percentage increase in R =R-R0R0×100=44%

subject: Physics
q: Twelve wires each having resistance $2 \Omega$ are joined to form a cube. A battery of $6 \mathrm{~V}$ emf is joined across point $a$ and $c$. The voltage difference between $e$ and $f$ is ______V.
question: Twelve wires each having resistance $2 \Omega$ are joined to form a cube. A battery of $6 \mathrm{~V}$ emf is joined across point $a$ and $c$. The voltage difference between $e$ and $f$ is ______V.
solution: From symmetry, current through e-b & g-d =0 $\begin{aligned} & \therefore \mathrm{R}_{\text {eq }}=\frac{3}{4} \times \mathrm{R}=\frac{3}{2} \Omega \\ & \therefore \text { Current through battery }=\frac{6 \times 2}{3}=4 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \mathrm{i}_2=\frac{4}{8} \times 2=1 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \therefore \Delta \mathrm{V} \text { across e-f }=\frac{\mathrm{i}_2}{2} \times \mathrm{R}=\frac{1}{2} \times 2=1 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : A uniform wire of resistance 80 Ω is cut into four equal parts. These parts are now connected in parallel. The equivalent resistance of the combination will be 5 Ω.

Statement II : Two resistance 2R and 3R are connected in parallel in an electric circuit. The value of thermal energy developed in 3R and 2R will be in the ratio 3:2.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : A uniform wire of resistance 80 Ω is cut into four equal parts. These parts are now connected in parallel. The equivalent resistance of the combination will be 5 Ω.

Statement II : Two resistance 2R and 3R are connected in parallel in an electric circuit. The value of thermal energy developed in 3R and 2R will be in the ratio 3:2.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
options: Both statement I and statement II are correct
Both statement I and statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but statement II is correct.
solution:
Statement 1- R=80 Ω

When it is cut into 4 equal parts, the resistance of each part will be,

R1=R2=R3=R4=20 Ω

When these resistances are connected in parallel,

Req =204=5 Ω

Hence, the statement I is correct.

Statement 2 -

When the resistances are connected in parallel, the potential difference between them is same. Therefore, the power consumed can be written as,

P=V2R

⇒P1P2=R2R1=23

Hence, statement II is incorrect.

subject: Physics
q: A battery of <math><mrow><mn>3.0</mn><mtext> V</mtext></mrow></math> is connected to a resistor dissipating <math><mrow><mn>0.5</mn><mtext> W</mtext></mrow></math> of power. If the terminal voltage of the battery is <math><mrow><mn>2.5</mn><mtext> V</mtext></mrow></math>, the power dissipated within the internal resistance is:
question: A battery of <math><mrow><mn>3.0</mn><mtext> V</mtext></mrow></math> is connected to a resistor dissipating <math><mrow><mn>0.5</mn><mtext> W</mtext></mrow></math> of power. If the terminal voltage of the battery is <math><mrow><mn>2.5</mn><mtext> V</mtext></mrow></math>, the power dissipated within the internal resistance is:
solution:
Given,

Emf of the battery, E=3 V

Potential difference across resister R is VR=2.5 V

Power dissipation in the resister is P=0.5 W

By using Kirchhoff's voltage law is given by,

Vr+VR=E  ...(1)

Vr is voltage across internal resister.

Using equation (1) and substitute the given values,

⇒Vr+2.5=3

⇒Vr=0.5

Now the ratio of potential across R and r is given by,

VRVr=IRIr=2.50.5=5

⇒Rr=5

Now the power dissipation across the internal resistance is given by

PRPr=I2RI2r=Rr

⇒PRPr=5

⇒Pr=PR5

⇒Pr=0.55=0.1W

subject: Physics
q: The equivalent resistance between the points $A$ and $B$ in the following circuit is $\frac{x}{5} \Omega$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: The equivalent resistance between the points $A$ and $B$ in the following circuit is $\frac{x}{5} \Omega$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Using Kirchhoff's laws on the bridge circuit with resistances $6\Omega, 3\Omega, 3\Omega, 3\Omega, 6\Omega$ and applying a 1V test voltage:

From the loop equation:
$6i_1 + 3(2i_1 - i) = 3(i - i_1)$

$15i_1 = 6i \Rightarrow i_1 = \frac{2}{5}i$ ...(1)

From the second loop:
$3(i - i_1) + 6i_1 = 1$
$3i + 3i_1 = 1$

$\left(3 + \frac{6}{5}\right)i = 1$

$i = \frac{5}{21}$ A $= \frac{1V}{R_{eq}}$

$R_{eq} = \frac{21}{5} = 4.2 \ \Omega$

What is the resistance of the given galvanometer?

0.98 I × 5 = 0.02 × RG

RG = 245 Ω

subject: Physics
q: Two resistors $2 \Omega$ and $3 \Omega$ are connected in the gaps of bridge as shown in figure. The null point is obtained with the contact of jockey at some point on wire $X Y$. When an unknown resistor is connected in parallel with $3 \Omega$ resistor, the null point is shifted by 22.5 cm toward $Y$. The resistance of unknown resistor is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
question: Two resistors $2 \Omega$ and $3 \Omega$ are connected in the gaps of bridge as shown in figure. The null point is obtained with the contact of jockey at some point on wire $X Y$. When an unknown resistor is connected in parallel with $3 \Omega$ resistor, the null point is shifted by 22.5 cm toward $Y$. The resistance of unknown resistor is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
solution:
In a Wheatstone bridge with 2Ω and 3Ω, the null point is found when $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{l}{L-l}$.
Initial balance: $\frac{2}{3} = \frac{l_1}{L-l_1}$ gives $l_1 = \frac{2L}{5}$.
For standard wire length $L = 100$ cm, $l_1 = 40$ cm.
When unknown resistance $R$ is connected in parallel with 3Ω, equivalent resistance becomes $\frac{3R}{3+R}$.
New null point: $\frac{2}{\frac{3R}{3+R}} = \frac{l_2}{L-l_2}$, which simplifies to $\frac{2(3+R)}{3R} = \frac{l_2}{L-l_2}$.
Null point shifts 22.5 cm toward Y: $l_2 = 40 + 22.5 = 62.5$ cm.
Substituting: $\frac{2(3+R)}{3R} = \frac{62.5}{37.5} = \frac{5}{3}$
$6(3+R) = 15R \Rightarrow 18 = 9R \Rightarrow R = 2$ Ω

subject: Physics
q: In the experiment of Ohm's law, a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is applied across the end of a conductor of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and diameter of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The measured current in the conductor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The maximum permissible percentage error in the resistivity of the conductor is :-
question: In the experiment of Ohm's law, a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is applied across the end of a conductor of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and diameter of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The measured current in the conductor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The maximum permissible percentage error in the resistivity of the conductor is :-
solution:
R=ρℓA=VI
ρ=AVIℓ=πd2 V4Iℓ  A=πd24
∴  Δρρ=2Δdd+ΔVV+ΔII+Δℓℓ
Δρρ=20.015.00+0.15.0+0.012.00+0.110.0
Δρρ=0.004+0.02+0.005+0.01
Δρρ=0.039
% error =Δρρ×100=0.039×100=3.90%.

subject: Physics
q: A rectangular parallelopiped is measured as 1 cm×1 cm×100 cm. If its specific resistance is 3×10-7Ω m, then the resistance between its two opposite rectangular faces will be _____×10-7Ω.
question: A rectangular parallelopiped is measured as 1 cm×1 cm×100 cm. If its specific resistance is 3×10-7Ω m, then the resistance between its two opposite rectangular faces will be _____×10-7Ω.
solution:
It is given that 

l = 1 cm = 0.01 m

A = 100 cm × 1 cm= 100 cm2

⇒A= 0.01 m2

ρ=3×10-7 Ω m

Using the formula of resistance,

R=ρlA=3×10-7×0.010.01=3×10-7Ω

q: Model a torch battery of length l to be made up of a thin cylindrical bar of radius a and a concentric thin cylindrical shell of radius b filled in between with an electrolyte of resistivity ρ (see figure). If the battery is connected to a resistance of value R, the maximum Joule heating in R will take place for :
question: Model a torch battery of length l to be made up of a thin cylindrical bar of radius a and a concentric thin cylindrical shell of radius b filled in between with an electrolyte of resistivity ρ (see figure). If the battery is connected to a resistance of value R, the maximum Joule heating in R will take place for :
solution:
Let a small elementary cylinder in the shell of thickness dr.

The resistance of a wire is given by R=ρLA.

Here ρ is the resistivity of the material.

The resistance of small elementdR=ρdr2πrl

Integrating both sides,

⇒R=ρ2πl∫abdrr

⇒R=ρ2πllnba

subject: Physics
q: Two identical cells, when connected either in parallel or in series gives same current in an external resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The internal resistance of each cell will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>.
question: Two identical cells, when connected either in parallel or in series gives same current in an external resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The internal resistance of each cell will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>.
solution:
Let r be the internal resistance of each cell.

In above shown figure, first represents the series connection of cells. Second and third figures represents the parallel connection of cells.

Now, in case of series connection, εnet=2ε and rnet=2r. So, current i=2ε5+2r .....(1)

In case of parallel connection, εnet=εr+εrr+r=ε and rnet=r2. So, current i=εr2+5 .....(2)

Equating (1) and (2), we get

2ε5+2r=εr2+5

⇒r+10=5+2r

⇒r=5 Ω.

subject: Physics
q: The actual value of resistance R , shown in the figure is 30Ω. This is measured in an experiment as shown using the standard formula R=VI , where V and I are the readings of the voltmeter and ammeter, respectively. If the measured value of R is 5% less, then the internal resistance of the voltmeter is:
question: The actual value of resistance R , shown in the figure is 30Ω. This is measured in an experiment as shown using the standard formula R=VI , where V and I are the readings of the voltmeter and ammeter, respectively. If the measured value of R is 5% less, then the internal resistance of the voltmeter is:
solution:
Measured value of R=5% less than
actual value of R i.e., R'=30-(5% of 30)=30-5100×30

R'=28.5 Ω ...i

Now, let us assume that internal resistance of voltmeter RV. Replacing voltmeter with its internal resistance, we get following circuit.

It is clear that the measured value, R' should be equal to parallel combination of R and RV. Mathematically,
R'=R×RVR+RV=28.5 Ω

Given. R=30 Ω

⇒ 30 RV30+RV=28.5

⇒ 30 RV=28.5×30+28.5 RV

⇒ 1.5 RV=28.5×30

⇒ RV=28.5×301.5=19×30

or RV=570 Ω

subject: Physics
q: Space between two concentric conducting spheres of radii <math><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math> <math><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mo>></mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is filled with a medium of resistivity <math><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">ρ</mi></math> . The resistance between the two spheres will be:
question: Space between two concentric conducting spheres of radii <math><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math> <math><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mo>></mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is filled with a medium of resistivity <math><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">ρ</mi></math> . The resistance between the two spheres will be:
solution:
Consider small volume element between r and r+dr
Resistance of this volume along rational directions,
dR=ρdr4πr2
As all element resistance are in series,
R=∫r=ar=bρdr4πr2=ρ4π-1rr=ar=b=ρ4π1a-1b

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: Alloys such as constantan and manganin are used in making standard resistance coils.

Reason R: Constantan and manganin have very small value of temperature coefficient of resistance.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: Alloys such as constantan and manganin are used in making standard resistance coils.

Reason R: Constantan and manganin have very small value of temperature coefficient of resistance.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.
A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.
solution:
The alloys manganin and constantan are used for making standard resistance coils as they have high resistivity, low-temperature coefficient of resistance, ability to attain good resistance values in small size. Their resistance value would change very little with temperature.

Thus, both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

subject: Physics
q:
A hollow cylindrical conductor has length of 3.14 m, while its inner and outer diameters are 4 mm and 8 mm respectively. The resistance of the conductor is n×10-3 Ω.
If the resistivity of the material is 2.4×10-8 Ω m. The value of n is _____.
question:
A hollow cylindrical conductor has length of 3.14 m, while its inner and outer diameters are 4 mm and 8 mm respectively. The resistance of the conductor is n×10-3 Ω.
If the resistivity of the material is 2.4×10-8 Ω m. The value of n is _____.
solution:
Resistance of a conductor with uniform cross-section is given by,

R=ρlA

In this case the cross-sectional area is πb2-a2.

Therefore,

R=ρlπb2-a2=2.4×10-8×3.143.14×42-22×10-6=2×10-3 Ω

Hence, n=2.

subject: Physics
q: The balancing length for a cell is <math><mn>560</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in a potentiometer experiment. When an external resistance of <math><mn>10</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>  is connected in parallel to the cell, the balancing length changes by <math><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> . If the internal resistance of the cell is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mtext>n</mtext><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> , where n is an integer then value of n is ________
question: The balancing length for a cell is <math><mn>560</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in a potentiometer experiment. When an external resistance of <math><mn>10</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>  is connected in parallel to the cell, the balancing length changes by <math><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> . If the internal resistance of the cell is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mtext>n</mtext><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> , where n is an integer then value of n is ________
solution:
Let the emf of cell is ε internal resistance is 'r' and potential gradient is x .
Only cell connected:
E=560x  -----1
After connecting the resistor
E×1010+r=500x   ------2
From 1 and 2
560x×1010+r=500x
56=50+5r
r=65=1.2Ω
so,n=12

q: A 220 volt, 1000 watt bulb is connected across a 110 volt mains supply. The power consumed will be
question: A 220 volt, 1000 watt bulb is connected across a 110 volt mains supply. The power consumed will be
solution:
$\mathrm{p}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^2}{\mathrm{R}}$ or $\mathrm{R}=\frac{(220)^2}{1000}$
Power consumed $=\frac{\mathrm{V}^2}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{110 \times 110}{220 \times 220} \times 1000=250$ watt

subject: Physics
q:
Paragraph: Consider a block of conducting material of resistivity ' $\rho$ ' shown in the figure. Current 'l' enters at 'A' and leaves from ' $\mathrm{D}$ '. We apply superposition principle to find voltage ' $\Delta \mathrm{V}$ ' developed between ' $\mathrm{B}$ ' and ' $\mathrm{C}$ '. The calculation is done in the following steps: (i) Take current 'l' entering from 'A' and assume it to spread over a hemispherical surface in the block. (ii) Calculate field $E(r)$ at distance ' $r$ ' from $A$ by using Ohm's law $E=\rho j$, where $j$ is the current per unit area at ' $r$ '. (iii) From the ' $r$ ' dependence of $E(r)$, obtain the potential $V(r)$ at $r$. (iv) Repeat (i), (ii) and (iii) for current 'l' leaving ' $D$ ' and superpose results for ' $A$ ' and ' $D$ '.
Question: $\Delta \mathrm{V}$ measured between $\mathrm{B}$ and $\mathrm{C}$ is
question:
Paragraph: Consider a block of conducting material of resistivity ' $\rho$ ' shown in the figure. Current 'l' enters at 'A' and leaves from ' $\mathrm{D}$ '. We apply superposition principle to find voltage ' $\Delta \mathrm{V}$ ' developed between ' $\mathrm{B}$ ' and ' $\mathrm{C}$ '. The calculation is done in the following steps: (i) Take current 'l' entering from 'A' and assume it to spread over a hemispherical surface in the block. (ii) Calculate field $E(r)$ at distance ' $r$ ' from $A$ by using Ohm's law $E=\rho j$, where $j$ is the current per unit area at ' $r$ '. (iii) From the ' $r$ ' dependence of $E(r)$, obtain the potential $V(r)$ at $r$. (iv) Repeat (i), (ii) and (iii) for current 'l' leaving ' $D$ ' and superpose results for ' $A$ ' and ' $D$ '.
Question: $\Delta \mathrm{V}$ measured between $\mathrm{B}$ and $\mathrm{C}$ is
solution:
Choosing $A$ as origin,
$$
\begin{gathered}
E=\rho j=\rho \frac{1}{2 \pi r^2} \\
V_C-V_B=-\frac{\rho l}{2 \pi} \int_a^{(a+b)} \frac{1}{r^2} d r=\frac{\rho l}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{1}{(a+b)}-\frac{1}{a}\right] \\
V_B-V_C=\frac{\rho l}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{1}{a}-\frac{1}{(a+b)}\right]
\end{gathered}
$$

q: A null point is found at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in potentiometer when cell in secondary circuit is shunted by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. When a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is used for shunting null point moves to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The internal resistance of the cell is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>.
question: A null point is found at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in potentiometer when cell in secondary circuit is shunted by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. When a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is used for shunting null point moves to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The internal resistance of the cell is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>.
solution:
Let potential gradient be x.

For the first case, we can write

εr+5×5=200x ......(1)

and after adding resistance, we can write

εr+15×15=300x .....(2)

From both equations, we get

⇒r=5 Ω

q: The value of current i1 flowing from A to C in the circuit diagram is :
question: The value of current i1 flowing from A to C in the circuit diagram is :
solution:
The Diagram shown in the question represents the balanced wheatstone bridge. It satisfy the condition of balanced wheat stone bridge.
Multiplication of dimensionally opposite resistance is same.
2×2=2×2⇒4=4
When the circuit is balanced wheat stone bridge then current through the branch BD is zero, so it can be removed.

Now, after removing BD wire voltage across AC is 8 V.

So, current will be,

i2=84+4=1 A

q:
With the help of potentiometer, we can determine the value of emf of a given cell. The sensitivity of the potentiometer is

(A) directly proportional to the length of the potentiometer wire

(B) directly proportional to the potential gradient of the wire

(C) inversely proportional to the potential gradient of the wire

(D) inversely proportional to the length of the potentiometer wire

Choose the correct option for the above statements:
question:
With the help of potentiometer, we can determine the value of emf of a given cell. The sensitivity of the potentiometer is

(A) directly proportional to the length of the potentiometer wire

(B) directly proportional to the potential gradient of the wire

(C) inversely proportional to the potential gradient of the wire

(D) inversely proportional to the length of the potentiometer wire

Choose the correct option for the above statements:
solution:
The basic principle of the potentiometer is that the potential drop across any section of the wire will be directly proportional to the length of the wire, provided the wire is of the uniform cross-sectional area and a uniform current flows through the wire.

Therefore, sensitivity of potentiometer wire is inversely proportional to potential gradient.

Sensitivity∝1Vl∝lV

subject: Physics
q: The resistance of the series combination of two resistances is $S$. When they are joined in parallel through total resistance is $P$. If $S=n P$, then the minimum possible value of $n$ is
question: The resistance of the series combination of two resistances is $S$. When they are joined in parallel through total resistance is $P$. If $S=n P$, then the minimum possible value of $n$ is
solution:
Let resistances be $R_1$ and $R_2$
So, $S=R_1+R_2$;
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{P}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_1 \mathrm{R}_2}{\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2} \\
& \mathrm{~S}=\mathrm{nP} \\
& \mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2=\frac{\mathrm{nR}_1 \mathrm{R}_2}{\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2} \\
& \left(\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2\right)^2=\mathrm{nR}_1 \mathrm{R}_2
\end{aligned}
$$
If $R_1=R_2$, so minimum value of $n=4$.

q: Five identical cells each of internal resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> are connected in series and in parallel with an external resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> For what value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> current in series and parallel combination will remain the same?
question: Five identical cells each of internal resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> are connected in series and in parallel with an external resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> For what value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> current in series and parallel combination will remain the same?
solution:
i1=5εR+5r   ...1

 

I2=εR+r5   ...2

From equation 1 and 2

i1=i2

5εR+5r=εR+r5⇒R=r=1Ω
